forecasts.reasoning: I start from the 0.225 empirical base rate for ACLED any-increase questions. I adjust upward because the benchmark is currently reported as 0.000, which likely means the operative threshold is effectively at least one ACLED-coded protest in the 30-day window. Sri Lanka-specific evidence shows protest capacity still exists in 2025: CIVICUS and other rights monitors report ongoing peaceful protests, including a Mannar protest continuing into mid-October 2025, plus student, civil-society, and labor protests earlier in 2025. The 2025-12-09 window also includes the late-November Maaveerar Naal period, a recurring point of Tamil mobilization and state surveillance/disruption. Offsetting this, the prompt’s zero ACLED baseline suggests actual ACLED-coded protest events have been very sparse, so non-ACLED reporting may overstate resolution-relevant activity. Net: all three dates are below 50%, but above the generic base rate; later horizons are pushed a bit closer to 0.5 due greater uncertainty and the November commemorative catalyst.

Finland’s threshold is likely about 11 fatalities in a 30-day window because the latest known reference value was 1.000. That is a very high bar for a country with almost no recent ACLED fatalities. The best evidence I found points to a nonzero but still low tail risk: Finland’s terrorism threat was assessed as elevated in 2025, with concern focused on lone actors or small extremist cells, and Finland has had isolated lethal or near-lethal school attacks. But the same official sources describe the increase as only slight, Russia-related activity mostly as sabotage/influence rather than lethal attacks, and I found no sign of an imminent multi-fatality plot or ongoing campaign. For 2025-11-16, most of the 30-day window had already effectively passed by the forecast date, so the chance of reaching 11+ fatalities is at the floor. I increase modestly for 2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07 because more future time remains for a rare tail event, but probabilities stay low because Finland remains an extremely low-conflict setting and ACLED may not code ordinary criminal violence.

Baseline is effectively zero, so this is close to asking whether ACLED will code at least one 'Violence against civilians' event in each 30-day window. Saint Vincent and the Grenadines clearly has a serious violent-crime problem: police described gun violence as a major problem, 2024 homicide totals were very high, gangs are reportedly active, and there was at least one homicide inside the first window. But the strongest contrary signal is that ACLED’s known 360-day reference average was still 0.000 as of 2025-10-30 despite all that homicide activity. That suggests most killings there are being treated as ordinary criminal/interpersonal violence, not ACLED-coded violence by organized armed groups against civilians. So I keep probabilities below the generic 0.225-ish prior for the near date, but not dramatically below because gang/organized-crime violence could still generate a qualifying event. I nudge later dates modestly toward 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty over a longer horizon.

This is effectively a question about whether Kuwait records any ACLED-counted fatality in each target 30-day window, because the known reference average is 0.000 and is unlikely to be revised high enough to require more than 1 death. Kuwait is a very low-violence case: recent reporting shows protests and political repression but no identified ACLED-relevant fatalities, and the one concrete 2024 militant case was foiled before any attack. Generic terrorism warnings and regional spillover risk keep the probability above the floor, especially for the more distant 2026 window. I therefore put the nearest date quite low, the December date slightly higher, and the February date higher still because more can change by then, while keeping all three below the generic prior given Kuwait’s strong security apparatus and long recent stretch without political-violence deaths.

Lithuania’s threshold is currently about 11+ ACLED-coded Battles in a 30-day window, because the most recent known reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-30. Everything I found before 2025-11-09 points to elevated tension but not ongoing combat: Zapad 2025 raised concern, there were drone incursions, brief airspace violations, and border-security measures, but no evidence of repeated armed-group clashes on Lithuanian soil. Top Lithuanian officials explicitly said the exercises did not signal imminent aggression and framed the main risk as provocations rather than invasion. So the only realistic YES pathway is a sudden, major Russia/Belarus–NATO escalation that produces many discrete ACLED 'Battles' very quickly. That tail risk is not zero, but it is small. The first date is lowest because much of its 30-day window was already effectively in the past by the cutoff and there was still no sign of battles; later dates are slightly higher only because they allow more time for a low-probability geopolitical shock.

French Polynesia starts from an extremely low-conflict baseline: the known reference value is 1.000, so a YES would require 11+ ACLED-coded Battles in the relevant 30-day window. The available pre-cutoff evidence points to a very peaceful, low-risk territory rather than one with active armed organizations: official advisories say exercise normal precautions / no recent terrorism history, and searches for clashes mostly surfaced New Caledonia instead of French Polynesia. The main pro-independence politics in French Polynesia appear institutional and electoral, not insurgent. So the only plausible YES path is a sudden, unforeseen militant or state-vs-group crisis. That is extremely unlikely over the next week, still extremely unlikely over a month, and only slightly less unlikely by early February, hence a modest upward drift with horizon.

I start from the empirical 0.225 base rate, then adjust down because Myanmar’s recent ACLED riot baseline is explicitly 0.000 as of 2025-10-30, so riots appear absent or extremely rare in the recent year. The available reporting and ACLED-based summaries describe Myanmar in 2025 as intensely violent, but mostly through battles, airstrikes, remote violence, and violence against civilians rather than crowd riots. That makes the key question effectively: will there be at least one ACLED-coded riot in each 30-day window? For 2025-11-16, much of the window is already partly observable from the forecast date and I found no clear sign of such an event, so I put it lowest. For 2025-12-09, I raise slightly for added uncertainty. For 2026-02-07, I raise further because the window includes the Feb. 1 coup anniversary, a recurring mobilization focal point, though past anniversaries have often taken the form of ‘silent strikes’ rather than street disorder, so I still keep the probability below the general base rate.

Albania looks like a very low-incidence setting for ACLED 'Violence against civilians.' The question itself says the latest known reference value on 2025-10-30 was 1.000, which strongly suggests zero such events in the preceding 360 days, so the operative threshold is likely 11+ events in a 30-day window. Searches found organized crime and occasional targeted killings, including an October 2025 courtroom shooting, but not an active insurgency, civil-conflict dynamic, or sustained campaign of attacks on civilians. That makes a sudden 11-event burst in the next week very unlikely, somewhat more possible over the next month, and still low but less remote by early February because there is more time for a shock to emerge. I therefore put all three forecasts at the floor/near-floor end, with modestly higher probabilities at longer horizons to reflect increased uncertainty and more opportunity for an unexpected violence wave.

Jordan’s battle baseline is essentially zero, and the latest known reference value was 1.000, so the hurdle is roughly 11 ACLED-coded Battles in a 30-day window. The evidence I found shows Jordan does have occasional armed border clashes with smugglers/infiltrators, including January 2024, March 2025, and August 2025 incidents, but these appear isolated rather than a sustained campaign. I found no sign of an Oct-Nov 2025 escalation already underway, which makes the 2025-11-16 date especially unlikely. I raise the probability slightly for later dates because there is more time for Syria-border deterioration or regional spillover to create a short burst of battle events, but even by 2026-02-07 it still looks clearly unlikely.

Argentina looks like a very low-base-rate case for ACLED 'Battles' spikes. The practical threshold is likely about 11+ battle-coded events in a 30-day window if the baseline stays near 1.0. Searches found persistent criminal violence and security operations in Rosario, but the strongest reporting points to a cooling trend rather than an escalating armed campaign, and I found no evidence of a late-Oct/early-Nov 2025 cluster of clashes that would make the 2025-11-16 window likely to clear the bar. Later dates get slightly higher probabilities only because there is more time for a sudden anti-gang offensive or Patagonia-related flare-up, plus ACLED classification uncertainty. My inside-view estimate is probably below 5% for the first date, but I am respecting the platform minimum.

I anchor on the provided 0.225 base rate for ACLED any-increase questions, then adjust upward modestly because Hungary only likely needs one ACLED-coded strategic-development event to beat the fixed baseline, which is currently known as 0.000 as of 2025-10-30 and is unlikely to be high enough after revisions to require more than one event. ACLED’s strategic-developments category includes non-violent but politically important events such as arrests, agreements, and other major contextual state actions, so election-period state moves or sovereignty/crackdown actions could qualify. That said, Hungary has shown very sparse recent coding in this category, which keeps probabilities below 50%. The 2025-11-16 window is lowest because most of that 30-day period had already elapsed by the forecast date without clear evidence of a qualifying event. Probabilities rise for December and especially February because the 2026 election approaches, TISZA is polling strongly, and the government’s media/NGO/sovereignty pressure creates more pathways for a qualifying strategic development, while still remaining a low-frequency event class.

Martinique is a low-conflict territory, and the generic 10x-spike base rate is already tiny. The main upward adjustment is the clear precedent of serious unrest in Sep-Oct 2024: violent cost-of-living protests, use of firearms, curfews, burned businesses, and at least one death. But that still falls far short of the likely threshold of roughly 11+ ACLED fatalities in a 30-day window. By 2025, Reuters coverage appears largely retrospective or focused on trials, negotiations, and grievances rather than an ongoing lethal conflict campaign, and the prompt’s reference value of 1.000 implies an extremely low recent fatal baseline. So the near-term risk is very low, with only a modest rise for later dates because persistent socioeconomic grievances could still reignite unrest over a longer horizon.

I start from the 0.225 empirical base rate for ACLED any-increase questions. I adjust upward because Indonesia had repeated lethal political violence in 2025, especially in Papua, including Reuters-reported killings in April, May, and mid-October, and ACLED-linked monthly trackers described Papua as an active conflict theater. I do not move much higher because the resolution compares each future 30-day window against the final 360-day average before 2025-11-09, and that baseline may be revised upward once ACLED backfills previously reported Papua fatalities; if so, the target is not merely 'any fatality' but 'beat a positive monthly average.' The first date is lower because most of its 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date and the clearest recent fatal event appears to have been on or just before Oct. 16, likely outside or only borderline for that window. Later dates are slightly higher but still below 0.5 because violence looks episodic rather than continuous; I also regress the more distant horizons modestly toward 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty.

The first date is lower because most of its 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date and the clearest recent fatal event appears to have been on or just before Oct. 16, likely outside or only borderline for that window. Later dates are slightly higher but still below 0.5 because violence looks episodic rather than continuous; I also regress the more distant horizons modestly toward 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty.

I start below the generic 0.225 ACLED any-increase base rate because Suriname is a low-conflict country and the market background says the latest known 360-day reference average on 2025-10-30 was 0.000, making this close to a simple 'will there be any ACLED-recorded fatality in the 30-day window?' question. The strongest country-specific evidence is negative: Suriname's main 2025 political stress test, the May election and July presidential selection, was reported as overall peaceful, and repeated targeted searches did not uncover any authoritative reporting of lethal unrest or escalating instability in late Oct/early Nov 2025. There are some reasons not to go too low: Suriname did have major anti-government riots in 2023, and mining/security frictions have produced violence and injuries in the recent past, so a surprise fatal event is not impossible. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, with no sign of deaths, so I put it lowest. For 2025-12-09 and especially 2026-02-07, I move probabilities upward toward 0.5 because more future time means more uncertainty and more opportunity for a rare fatal event.

Nicaragua looks like a classic low-base-rate, low-mobilization case. The strongest country-specific fact is that the reference value was still 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, implying essentially zero ACLED-coded riots over the prior 360 days. Recent Reuters/OHCHR/Confidencial reporting depicts an entrenched police state with surveillance, raids, disappearances, and suppression of opposition rather than active street confrontation. I also found no convincing evidence of contemporaneous late-2025 protests or clashes that would signal a rising riot wave. For 2025-11-16, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, so getting to 11+ riots would require an abrupt surge in just days. I raise the probability slightly for 2025-12-09 and again for 2026-02-07 only because longer horizons allow more room for an unforeseen trigger, and Nicaragua has shown in the past that latent grievances can erupt quickly. Even so, current conditions make a 10x spike unlikely.

Base rate for 10x ACLED spikes is already tiny, and North Macedonia’s specific baseline is even quieter: the latest known reference value was 1.000, so the question likely needs about 11+ Explosions/Remote violence events in a 30-day window. My searches found elevated terrorism concern, arrests, and a medium threat level, but not an actual ongoing bombing/insurgent campaign. Recent prominent 'bomb' incidents were mostly hoaxes, and targeted searches for 2024-2025 real explosions mainly surfaced weapons seizures, prevention, or historical ordnance rather than repeated attacks. So a YES would require a sharp, sudden break from the near-zero recent pattern. I therefore keep the nearest date at the floor-like minimum, with only modest increases for later dates because there is more time for a new campaign or regional spillover to emerge.

This looks like an extreme-tail event. The Falklands are a real geopolitical flashpoint in principle, but the bar for YES is very high: with the known reference value near 1.0, the outcome likely requires at least 11 ACLED-coded battle events in a 30-day window. Searches found diplomacy, UN calls for a peaceful solution, and even UK-Argentina military dialogue—not active hostilities. The only live path to YES is a sudden operational crisis (naval/air incident or rapid interstate escalation). That is especially unlikely by 2025-11-16 because, absent hidden events before 2025-11-09, it would likely require an implausibly intense burst in the final week. I therefore give very low probabilities, increasing modestly with horizon.

Canada is a low-conflict setting for ACLED 'Battles', so I adjust down from the generic 0.225 any-increase prior. The main path to YES is organized-crime conflict: reporting in 2024-25 described active turf wars involving Quebec street gangs, Hells Angels, and other organized-crime actors, plus ongoing gang conflict in Metro Vancouver (search_4_result_2, search_2_result_0, search_6_result_9). But most concrete late-2025 incidents I found were targeted killings and heavy police crackdowns rather than a clear two-sided armed clash, and I found no obvious qualifying clash already inside most of the first window (search_2_summary). So 2025-11-16 is fairly low, while the later dates are modestly higher because they cover less-observed future windows; they remain well below 50% because Canada rarely records battles and ACLED's coding standard is narrower than general gang violence.

The base rate for 10x-spike ACLED questions is extremely low, and Malawi’s latest known reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-30, implying the hurdle is probably about 11 riot events in a 30-day window. I found meaningful background risk factors: severe economic stress, earlier 2025 protests in major cities, documented attacks on protesters, and security-force warnings that election-related unrest was possible. But the strongest date-specific evidence points the other way: Malawi’s riot baseline had remained near zero over the prior year, targeted searches did not uncover reports of widespread riot activity in October-November 2025, and the most important possible catalyst—the September 2025 election—appears to have been defused by Chakwera’s concession, with reporting emphasizing acceptance/tight security rather than a sustained post-election street conflict. So the near-date probability stays at the floor. I raise the later dates modestly because more time allows for fresh economic or political triggers, but there is still no concrete evidence as of 2025-11-09 for an imminent 11+ riot burst.

Finland is a very low-conflict setting, and the known reference average near the cutoff was 0.000, so this question is effectively about whether ACLED records at least one qualifying 'Violence against civilians' event in each 30-day window. The main upside risk is that Finnish intelligence kept the terror threat elevated and warned that extreme-right danger had grown, with some signs of concrete preparation, and Finland did see at least one ideologically linked anti-civilian stabbing in 2024. But the stronger near-term evidence is still negative: a Finland-specific 2025 assessment said a large-scale attack was unlikely and emphasized lone actors/small groups rather than robust organized cells; the notable July 2025 Tampere stabbing was treated by police as random and non-political; and I found no clear late-2025 Finland-specific extremist attack or disrupted plot before the 2025-11-09 cutoff. That leaves a low monthly hazard. I put the first date lowest because most of that window had already elapsed by the cutoff and no qualifying event surfaced; later dates are slightly higher only because uncertainty rises with horizon.

I anchored on the FRED tool’s statistical estimate for the actual series. As of 2025-11-07, AAA10Y was 1.16, and the tool’s trend/seasonal model estimated only a 0.34 probability of being above 1.16 by 2025-11-16. I keep the first-date forecast very close to that anchor. The one permitted market-context search modestly reinforces a below-50% view: late-2025 commentary says investment-grade corporate spreads tightened in Q3, were near very tight historical levels, and strong investor demand was supporting tighter spreads rather than wider ones. That argues against a sustained rise in the Aaa-vs-10Y spread from the current elevated level. For the later dates, I move probabilities closer to 0.5 because horizon-driven uncertainty rises, even though the balance of evidence still slightly favors no increase over the baseline.

Primary anchor is the FRED statistical estimate, which put the 2025-11-16 probability near 0.49 using data through 2025-11-07 against a threshold of about 4.84. I nudge that slightly upward because market context right before the cutoff pointed to somewhat higher bond yields: Treasury yields had bounced back to around 4.16% on the 10-year, Powell said a December cut was not assured, strong payrolls reduced odds of aggressive easing, and some commentary pointed to sticky inflation, stronger growth, fiscal pressure, and some credit stress/downgrades. Those forces can lift both Treasury yields and corporate spreads, pushing the ICE BofA corporate yield up. Offsetting that, the series had mostly been trending down through 2025 before the late rebound, recent momentum was not decisively strong, the exact 2025-11-09 comparison value is unknown and may be a few basis points above 4.84, and some forecasts still expected lower Treasury yields into 2026. So I see only a very small edge toward 'up' in the short run, fading to a coin flip by February.

I anchor the near-term forecast to the FRED tool’s statistical estimate: with the last observed value at 0.84 on 2025-11-07, it estimated only about a 0.35 chance of being above that level by 2025-11-16. Qualitatively, strong inflows and risk appetite have kept IG spreads very tight, which argues against an immediate rise. But those same tight levels also create medium-term widening risk if volatility, tariffs/inflation concerns, or growth worries re-emerge, so I move the later probabilities gradually back toward 0.5 as horizon increases.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s statistical estimate: using data through 2025-11-05, it gives about a 0.69 probability that cash assets exceed the current threshold (~2891) by 2025-11-16. That is plausible because the latest level is unusually low versus most of 2025, so some rebound/mean reversion is more likely than not. I trim that a bit because recent weekly momentum was sharply down and there are reserve-draining forces from Treasury issuance/TGA rebuilding; for later dates I move probabilities closer to 0.5 to reflect growing uncertainty, though the Fed’s late-October decision to end balance-sheet runoff starting Dec. 1 is a modest supportive factor for bank cash assets.

Primary anchor is the FRED trend analysis: for the nearest date it gives a combined P(increase) of 0.55, with a modest positive trend and slightly positive recent momentum. I keep 2025-12-09 only a bit above 0.5 because the recent series trend is upward, but external credit reports suggest 2025 balance growth is slowing. I move 2026-02-07 back to essentially a coin flip/slight lean NO because the sampled prior-year path dropped materially in Jan-Feb 2025 and search results indicate post-holiday softening and elevated delinquencies. I also account for the fact that the comparison point is basically the current level, so even a small downtick makes the answer NO. Per instructions, uncertainty increases with horizon, so the later probabilities are closer to 0.5.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s own estimate: for the nearest horizon it gives a 0.35 chance that the TGA exceeds the latest known pre-cutoff level, which I interpret as the relevant comparison level as of 2025-11-09 (the 2025-11-05 weekly observation, 942.7B). Treasury’s own November financing guidance points to an end-December cash balance target of 850B and an end-March target of 850B, both below that threshold, which argues against sustained increases by early December or February (search_0_summary). Offsetting that, recent momentum has been upward, late-November bill sizes are expected to be maintained, the November refunding is net cash-positive, and Treasury expects to raise bill sizes again in mid-January, all of which keep open a path back above the threshold (fetch_ts_fred; search_0_summary). So I keep the 11/16 forecast near the FRED prior, then move modestly toward 0.5 at longer horizons because uncertainty rises and financing operations could temporarily lift the balance even if official quarter-end targets are lower.

My primary anchor is the FRED time-series analysis for DTB1YR through 2025-11-07, which estimated a 0.61 chance of being above 3.49 by 2025-11-16. That supports a modestly-better-than-even chance of an increase in the near term. I anchor on the fact that ECBDFR is a policy-controlled step function, not a smooth economic time series. The FRED tool’s 0.71 mechanical estimate is therefore misleading here: the rate had been flat at 2.0 since June 2025 after a long cutting cycle, and any increase requires an actual ECB policy decision. Reuters reported the ECB held the deposit rate at 2.00% on Oct. 30 for a third straight meeting, with inflation around target and the outlook broadly unchanged (search_0_result_0). A Reuters poll also found 72% of economists expected no change through end-2025, and cited that the hurdle for ECB hikes is usually high (search_0_result_1). Given the ECB’s meeting-based process and official calendars for 2025/2026 (search_0_result_2) (search_0_result_5), the first two resolution dates appear to fall before any ordinary opportunity to hike, leaving mostly only a very rare inter-meeting move. By 2026-02-07 there is more time and likely one or two scheduled meetings, so the chance of being above 2.0 rises somewhat, but consensus still points strongly to a pause rather than renewed tightening.

I anchor on the fetched EFFR time-series model, which estimated about a 0.62 chance that the rate would be above the latest observed pre-cutoff level (~3.87) by 2025-11-16. I shade that down because EFFR is mostly pinned by the Fed’s target range and usually moves in step changes around FOMC decisions, not by a smooth trend. There is no policy decision before 11/16, and the 12/09 resolution date is the first day of the Dec. 9-10 FOMC meeting, so those two dates are mostly about small technical drift around the current regime. By 2026-02-07, however, the question sits after the December meeting (and likely after a January meeting), and contemporaneous reporting said markets and economists still leaned toward another December cut after the late-October cut. That makes an increase versus 2025-11-09 materially less likely by February, though not negligible because Powell’s remarks also left a meaningful hold/no-cut path and the 2026 path was described as unclear.

Primary anchor is the required FRED trend output, which pointed to a modestly-better-than-even chance of being above the comparison level by 2025-12-09. I trim that a bit because the series had recent downward momentum and, importantly, the exact 2025-11-09 reference value was not known at forecast time. The one web search adds only mild upside pressure: late-2025 inflation was still described as stubbornly above target, tariff pass-through was still present, and markets had dialed back rapid rate-cut expectations, all of which slightly favor higher short-run expected inflation. But tariff pass-through was also said to be more modest than feared, and data disruptions/noise make this monthly series hard to call. Net: slight lean YES for December, then a smaller lean by February with extra uncertainty and mean reversion/shrinkage toward 0.5.

Primary anchor is the FRED time-series output: EXPINF5YR has been almost flat over the past year, with a slight negative linear trend, and the tool’s model gives a 0.44 chance of being above the 2025-11-01 level by 2025-12-09. I treat that as the starting point. I then nudge slightly upward because the one allowed search found mildly inflationary late-October/early-November context: Cleveland Fed/MNI material pointed to firmer CPI/PCE nowcasts, and Reuters described inflation pressures as firming with tariff pass-through still present and broader expectation measures in the 2.4%-3.0% range. That said, this is a sticky monthly 5-year expectation series, and if the relevant 2025-11-09 baseline is already the early-November reading around 2.331, the hurdle is a bit higher than the stale 2.320 late-October figure. So I end a bit above the pure trend model, but still below 50% for the near date. For the farther date, I move slightly closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty and the tendency of medium-term inflation expectations to wander around a narrow band rather than trend strongly month to month.

I anchor on the FRED time-series estimate for the nearest date: the model’s combined probability of exceeding the 2025-11-09 comparison value by 2025-11-16 is 0.45. That fits the recent pattern: momentum was flat and the series had eased from September highs, which argues against a strong near-term rise. Reuters reporting near the cutoff also described claims as broadly stable and noted a drop to 219k in late October, which keeps the first-date forecast below 50%. Against that, Reuters also reported a softer labor market, higher unemployment, a pickup in layoffs, and sharper announced job cuts in October, which modestly raises the odds that the 4-week average drifts back above the baseline by early December and especially by early February. Because uncertainty should rise with horizon, I only move the later probabilities slightly toward 0.5 rather than making a strong directional call.

The required FRED pull is the main anchor: the series has fallen from 1.84 to 1.64 over the last year, recent momentum is still net downward, and the tool’s short-horizon model gives only a 0.41 chance of being above 1.64 by 2025-12-09. I adjust that down because the question’s comparison point on 2025-11-09 is uncertain: if the 11/09 baseline ends up effectively still being the last then-known 1.680 rather than the later-published 1.64 area, an ‘increase’ is materially harder. I anchor primarily on the FRED time-series tool: the Cleveland Fed 10-year real rate series showed a mild but persistent downtrend over the prior year, recent momentum was mostly downward, and the tool’s combined estimate for being above the current threshold by 2025-12-09 was 0.30. I keep the first probability very close to that anchor. I nudge it only slightly upward because contemporaneous market reporting suggested 10-year Treasury yields were expected to stay elevated around 4.1% over the next 3-6 months, with term premium still high, which offers some support to the real-rate estimate. Offsetting that, markets were strongly pricing further Fed easing and inflation had recently come in a bit softer, both of which lean against a rise in the Cleveland Fed real-rate estimate. For 2026-02-07 I move somewhat closer to 0.5 because forecast uncertainty is higher at the longer horizon and there is more time for the series to rebound above the 2025-11-09 level, but the probability remains below even due to the underlying negative trend and policy-easing backdrop.

Primary anchor is the SNDR time-series itself: the FRED tool estimates only a 30% chance that the series is above 0.400 by 2025-12-09. The series drifted down over the past year (0.43 to 0.40), even though it stabilized recently. Macro evidence also leans against an increase: the Fed cut rates on 2025-10-29, and savings/CD yields generally fade during easing cycles. The main offset is that Powell said another December cut was not assured, so the near-term path is not one-way down and the series is sticky enough to stay flat or occasionally tick up. For the later date I move slightly closer to 0.5 to reflect higher uncertainty, but still keep the probability below 50% because the broader policy backdrop remains easing rather than tightening.

SOFR is a policy-sensitive overnight rate, so I anchor on the FRED tool’s quantitative estimate rather than the generic FRED base rate. The tool gives about a 0.69 chance that SOFR on 2025-11-16 is above the last observed 2025-11-07 value of 3.93, and the late-October/early-November drop looks unusually sharp relative to the preceding days. I trim that down because the resolution compares to the unknown 2025-11-09 value rather than exactly 3.93, and Reuters reporting indicates the Fed had just cut 25 bp, ended QT / added liquidity, and left December easing possible, all of which cap upside and make later horizons less favorable. So I see a modestly-better-than-even chance of an increase by 11/16, still above even by 12/09, and only slightly above even by 2026-02-07 after more time for policy easing.

I anchor on the FRED time-series output, which gives a 0.45 probability that the next observed weekly value exceeds the latest known threshold of 7.138B. The series is elevated but choppy, with recent readings bouncing between roughly 6.0B and 7.9B. One targeted news search adds a modest upward tilt: Reuters reported mid-October funding strain, elevated repo rates, and sizable Standing Repo Facility use, while broader liquidity was described as gradually tightening into late 2025. That supports somewhat higher odds that primary credit remains elevated into early December. Offsetting that, primary credit is mainly for temporary funding shortfalls, and the comparison level is already high, so further increases are still slightly less likely than not. For the longer horizon I move closer to 0.5, reflecting greater uncertainty and the possibility of either renewed stress or normalization.
