I estimate a 16% chance. The outside view favors some nontrivial chance because BIS has tightened AI-related export controls repeatedly in recent years. But by the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the main broad 2025 AI rule (the January AI Diffusion framework covering advanced chips and model weights) had been rescinded/non-enforced in May rather than expanded, and repeated targeted searches did not uncover any later broad Federal Register rule reimposing model-weight controls, adding cloud-compute controls, or globally expanding advanced-chip controls. The official BIS activity I found after May was mostly guidance or China-specific loophole/entity actions, which do not satisfy this question. So YES still requires a fairly fast late-2025 replacement rule or other broad interim final rule in the remaining weeks of 2025—possible, but not the likeliest outcome.

I estimate a 5% chance. By 2025-11-09, essentially all standalone AI-regulatory bills that clearly might qualify (NO FAKES, AI LEAD, COPIED) were still only introduced/referred to committee, and the most prominent House AI provision—the moratorium on state AI laws—had been crushed in the Senate. The only plausible remaining path is the FY2026 NDAA: both chambers had advanced/passed versions and legal summaries indicate some binding AI-specific defense provisions, but it is quite uncertain whether those provisions survive conference and, crucially, whether RFI would count internal defense-sector AI restrictions as 'substantive regulation' of AI systems or development.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, every authoritative description I found still framed the coalition’s force as a post-ceasefire/post-hostilities mission, not an active 2025 deployment. Reuters and official late-October statements emphasized that Russia had not accepted a full ceasefire and that coalition activity was focused on planning, winter support, economic pressure, and future guarantees. There is real coalition intent—26 countries reportedly pledged some ground/sea/air role—but the pathway to a qualifying deployment by Dec. 31 looked extremely narrow. Residual probability comes from a sudden ceasefire plus very rapid implementation, or a limited qualifying air/sea mission under coalition command.

An official German Defense Ministry announcement from May 10, 2024 says Ukraine is to receive three HIMARS from U.S. stocks paid for by Germany, and Defense News independently reported Pistorius saying Germany will buy three HIMARS launchers from U.S. stocks and give them to Ukraine. That appears to meet the resolution criteria directly: a European government formally announced a commitment to provide M142 HIMARS to Ukraine, well before 31 Dec 2025. The only residual uncertainty is a possible unforeseen interpretation quirk about whether pre-2025 commitments count, but the wording says 'by 31 December 2025,' which strongly suggests they do.

This looks very unlikely. By 2025, Russian pipeline gas to the EU was effectively limited to TurkStream after Ukraine transit ended, and Reuters-calculated TurkStream flows were only about 4.5 bcm in Q1 and 9.93 bcm in H1 2025 total. Separate reporting indicated Russian LNG to Europe in Jan-Aug 2025 was only around 9.2 million tons, roughly low-teens bcm over eight months, or about 4-5 bcm per quarter. Adding pipeline plus LNG gives a rough 2025 quarterly total near 9-10 bcm, well below the 15 bcm threshold. Hitting 15 bcm in any quarter would likely require either a major route reopening or an exceptional LNG spike; I found no evidence of either by the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff. Some factors push upward—Russia remained a meaningful supplier and the EU delayed immediate LNG restrictions—but not enough to overcome the structural loss of Ukraine transit and the observed 2025 flow levels.

Iran had real opportunity and motive during the June 2025 Israel-Iran crisis, and maritime disruption did rise sharply, but even then traffic was not halted. By early November, the evidence points to continued threats, naval signaling, seizure risk, and GNSS/AIS interference—not a transition to actual severe restriction of passage. Because the resolution criteria are broad, including selective interdiction of Western shipping, I keep a nontrivial tail risk. But with less than two months left, no observed move from rhetoric/harassment to closure, strong self-deterrence from Iran's own export dependence, and the near certainty of major U.S./allied response, YES looks unlikely. My estimate is 8%.

By 2025-11-09, the short-horizon outside view already strongly favored Musk staying CEO through year-end. The most concrete identified path to his departure was the board’s warning that he could leave if shareholders rejected his massive compensation package, but shareholders approved it on Nov. 6. Reuters reporting also indicates the board’s succession planning was largely contingency planning around that risk, while the board remained publicly supportive of Musk. Earlier 2025 reporting about CEO-search activity and Musk’s fatigue means the risk was not literally zero, but absent a fresh legal/health/emergency shock, ceasing to be CEO before 2025-12-31 looked unlikely. I would be slightly below 5% absent platform guidance floors, so I submit 5%.

As of 2025-11-09, the strongest consistent pre-cutoff evidence points to Leclerc still being ahead of Hamilton with only three rounds left. Independent standings reports earlier in the autumn had Leclerc well clear — e.g. 173 to 127 after Singapore (search_3_result_3), and Hamilton was reported 46 points behind after Monza (search_3_result_6). Late-season race evidence also moved against Hamilton: Leclerc beat him heavily in Mexico (18 points vs 4; search_0_result_6) and outscored him again in the Sao Paulo Sprint (search_1_result_6), while both then DNFed the GP. Crash’s post-Sao Paulo standings framing had Hamilton battling Antonelli for sixth, implying Leclerc was still ahead in the top five (search_3_result_1). With only Las Vegas, Qatar Sprint+GP, and Abu Dhabi remaining, Hamilton still had a mathematical path, but it likely required a very large late swing and/or Leclerc retirements. A single contradictory summary suggesting Hamilton was ahead is outweighed by the broader, more coherent evidence set plus the 0.06 market price on 2025-10-30. So YES is possible but only barely.

I estimate a 6% chance of YES. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the strongest pro-YES evidence was the June 2025 direct clash: the US struck Iranian nuclear sites, Iran retaliated against a US base, and major outlets described an intense 12-day confrontation. But the stronger evidence for the market’s specific resolution standard points the other way: US officials explicitly said they did not seek war, Trump moved quickly to announce a ceasefire and pursue talks, and by early November Reuters was describing sanctions pressure and Iranian inquiries about sanctions relief rather than an active, sustained US-Iran war. Given the creator’s clarified bar—more than a single strike, more like sustained campaigning/ground conflict or broad public acknowledgement of active war—the June episode looks more like a brief limited exchange than a resolved YES. That leaves only about seven weeks for a new sustained war to begin before year-end. That is possible if enrichment resumes or a proxy/Israeli trigger spirals, but historically still unlikely. The 0.04 market estimate on 2025-10-30 is directionally consistent with this low-risk view, though I’m a bit higher because of the demonstrated June precedent and lingering escalation risk.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the substantive case for YES is essentially gone. Under the stated rules, the market uses initial GDP estimates and requires two consecutive negative quarters both in 2025. Q1 2025 was negative, but Q2 2025's advance estimate was clearly positive at about +3.0%, so Q1-Q2 did not qualify (search_0_result_6). A later BEA release showed Q2 was even stronger in subsequent estimates, confirming it was not borderline (search_0_result_0). Most importantly, BEA reporting indicates Q3 2025 real GDP increased +4.1% on the relevant estimate, so Q3 was positive as well (search_1_result_3; search_1_summary). That means neither Q2-Q3 nor Q3-Q4 can be two consecutive negative quarters in 2025. Economically, this makes YES impossible absent some source/retrieval ambiguity, so I assign the minimum allowed 5% rather than zero.

By 2025-11-09, NBER had not declared any 2023 or 2024 recession, and its business-cycle chronology still showed the most recent peak as February 2020. Historically, NBER announced recent recession starts about 4 months (2020), 8 months (2001), and 12 months (2007) after the peak, so the lack of a declaration by November 2025 is strong evidence against any 2023 or early/mid-2024 recession. The main remaining tail risk is a late-2024 peak that NBER might still announce before end-2025. That tail is kept alive by some 2024 weakness and a Sahm-rule breach, but multiple 2025 analyses based on NBER-style indicators said the U.S. was not in recession and had avoided one in late 2024. Net: very unlikely, but not impossible.

I estimate a 5% chance. The barrier is close in absolute terms—the official world record is 2:00:35—and Valencia on Dec. 7 is a fast final opportunity. But the strongest negative is current form: the best active contenders in 2025 have been running in the 2:02–2:03 range, with Chicago’s serious record attempt still ending at 2:02:23. With Kelvin Kiptum deceased, the runner most plausibly positioned to close the final gap is gone. So YES requires an extraordinary one-race leap by someone like Kiplimo or Sawe at Valencia, which is possible but unlikely.

I estimate an 8% chance. By the Nov. 9 cutoff, I found no evidence of any qualifying post-April 4 repeat crash day. The main later selloffs were materially smaller: April 10 was -3.46% and October 10 was -2.71%, and Reuters described early-November markets as having reached record highs in recent months. With only about seven weeks left in 2025, a >5% close-to-close S&P 500 drop would likely require a fresh acute shock. That tail risk is real—especially given 2025’s tariff-driven volatility—but still modest.

Official Epoch evidence by Oct 2025 puts the best FrontierMath Tiers 1-3 score around 27-29%, with Tier 4 around 10-13%, and Epoch’s own analysis says even repeated runs of current models likely cap below 70%. Going from ~29% in early Nov to >80% by year-end would require an extraordinary, near-immediate breakthrough or a very favorable interpretation of what counts as ‘the benchmark.’ That seems possible but unlikely, so I’m at the floor: 5%.

I estimate a 5% chance. The 49ers were still clearly live at 6-3 around the cutoff, but the best available contemporaneous signals put them in the second tier of contenders rather than among the favorites. By early November they had slipped to 13th overall / 7th in the NFC in Super Bowl odds and trailed both the Seahawks and Rams in the division race, while model-based estimates earlier in the season were only about 4% to 6.2%. Injury news near the cutoff is the biggest drag: Brock Purdy remained limited/questionable and Mac Jones was set to start again in Week 10, which caps playoff-path and title upside. So I land a bit below the original 7% market anchor, at the low end of plausible contender probabilities.

I put this in the low-to-mid teens. By the cutoff, authoritative reporting showed Bitcoin’s 2025 peak was only about $125k-$126k, so $150k still required roughly another 20% from the prior high, not just a small breakout. Late-October analysis centered on resistance around $117k-$120k and a bullish near-term range of roughly $120k-$140k, which is supportive but still below the target. Offsetting that, ETF inflows and institutional demand were very strong in Q4, so a blowoff move was still plausible. Net: possible, but clearly not the base case.

Disney/ESPN and the NFL appear to have a real deal in principle, and NFL owners approved it in October. But the most authoritative timing evidence cuts the other way for a 2025 deadline: the August announcement was described as non-binding and subject to definitive agreements, approvals, and closing conditions; Reuters reported regulatory approval was still required and that DOJ was expected to conduct a substantive antitrust review; and SBJ reported the deal may not close until late 2026. With only about seven weeks left as of the knowledge cutoff and no clear evidence that Disney could legally exercise interim control before closing, I think year-end 2025 control is less likely than the market implied, though still plausible if approvals move unusually fast or 'control' is interpreted loosely.

My estimate is 15%. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, I found no clear evidence that a Waymo sketch had already aired, despite multiple targeted searches. That matters because if it had happened, it likely would have surfaced. On the other hand, Waymo was genuinely in the late-2025 zeitgeist: it was large, consumer-facing, expanding, and involved in several viral/safety stories. So the chance is not negligible. Still, SNL has only a limited number of remaining 2025 episodes after the cutoff, and any one specific company is an unusual sketch target. Net: low-but-real chance, roughly in line with the market.

Chrome is overwhelmingly likely to finish 2025 as the most popular browser. The best pre-cutoff evidence puts Chrome at roughly two-thirds of global all-browser usage, with Safari a distant second and Edge far behind. Over a horizon of only ~7 weeks, a leadership change from such a large gap is extremely unlikely. I stop short of >95% because the exact Wikipedia table/source snapshot has some methodological ambiguity, but the competitive risk looks minimal.

I estimate a 10% chance of YES. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Musk had clearly announced the 'America Party' in July and some outlets described that as a launch, but the stronger pattern is that the effort did not develop visible official infrastructure. Reports indicated uncertainty about whether the party actually existed, fake/inauthentic FEC-related filings, and late-July reporting that it was 'failing to launch.' Searches for August-November follow-through turned up little concrete evidence of an official website, official party account, or candidate/organizational rollout tied to Musk. With only about seven weeks remaining until year-end, and given the creator's clarification that a tweet alone is insufficient, NO is much more likely. I keep it above the market's 5% because Musk could still create a minimally recognizable party very quickly if he chose to.

I’d put this at 71%. The most important consideration is recency: by late October, multiple outlets reported a clear thaw, with Trump himself saying the relationship was 'good,' that he had been chatting with Musk again, and that he liked him. For a question about the state at the end of 2025, that near-cutoff evidence is highly diagnostic. The main reason not to go higher is that the earlier rupture was extremely severe and late-October business conflicts with Trump administration officials showed the détente was fragile rather than fully restored. With about seven weeks remaining from the cutoff, I think 'on good terms' is more likely than not, but far from certain.

By Nov. 9, the strongest pattern is rhetorical outrage without South-Park-specific legal follow-through. Trump and the White House clearly noticed and disliked the satire, and Trump has shown a broader willingness to pressure Paramount. But legal experts uniformly viewed the episode as core protected parody, making a real suit weak; no documented cease-and-desist or lawsuit had surfaced through months of coverage; Paramount's new leadership publicly praised the show; and South Park was still mocking Trump in a Nov. 1 episode without obvious retaliation. With less than two months left, a late cease-and-desist or regulatory move is still possible, but it looks like a tail risk rather than the central scenario.

I put this at 5%. By 2025-11-09 Iran faced real stress—renewed UN sanctions, recession risk, labor unrest, wartime damage, and Khamenei succession concerns—but the evidence available up to that date still pointed much more strongly to regime resilience than imminent collapse. The opposition/protest landscape appeared fragmented, security forces remained cohesive and heavily deployed, analysts judged collapse a distant prospect absent a domestic uprising or invasion, and even in late Oct./early Nov. the regime was still making senior military appointments and conducting nationwide readiness inspections. With only the final weeks of 2025 remaining, regime change required a fast-moving break in coercive control that there was little concrete evidence had begun.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the realized/near-realized GDP path makes a YES resolution unlikely. Q1 2025 was negative, but Q2 2025 was strongly positive at 3.8%, which already broke any Q1-Q2 recession sequence. Contemporaneous evidence for Q3 2025 was also clearly positive: Treasury said growth had 'solidified,' the WSJ survey median was +2.7%, and Fed nowcasts were around +2.4% to +3.9%. So under the normal 'by end of 2025' reading, YES would mostly require an unexpectedly negative Q3 plus a negative Q4, a low-probability combo. I keep a small residual probability because of late-2025 downside risks (shutdown/tariffs) and some ambiguity over whether a Q4 2025-Q1 2026 pair could count under the literal wording.

I estimate a 5% chance. The strongest evidence is negative: in March 2025 Disney/ABC said it was winding down FiveThirtyEight and dissolving the 538 brand, not merely pausing it; the legacy site appears to remain archived and not updated; and I found no authoritative late-2025 reporting of a sale, spinout, licensing deal, or revival attempt. ABC also appears to be maintaining polling content under other branding rather than 538. The remaining YES paths are still possible because the resolution criteria are generous — Disney could do a token revival, or a buyer like Nate Silver or another outlet could strike a quick deal and relaunch some IP before year-end — but with less than two months left as of the forecast date, and no visible public movement, that looks like a low-probability tail event rather than a live process.

I estimate a low but non-trivial chance. The strongest outside-view fact is the very short remaining timeline from the Nov. 9, 2025 forecast date to year-end; launching a legally usable real-money or crypto betting product usually requires compliance, payments/KYC, jurisdiction selection, and product rollout, and I found no concrete pre-cutoff evidence of a live beta or announced 2025 launch. Public descriptions still characterized Manifold as play-money using Mana (search_5_result_1; search_0_result_0), while broader 2025 reporting highlighted ongoing legal ambiguity for prediction/event-contract products that resemble gaming (search_4_result_2). On the positive side, founder statements over time show real, persistent interest in bringing back crypto/real-money functionality (search_0_result_2; search_4_result_6), and the resolution bar is relatively low because even one legal jurisdiction or affiliated platform would count. Still, years of interest without a visible launch, plus no concrete evidence by Nov. 9 with only weeks left, keep the probability well below the market midpoint.

I put this slightly above even. The strongest official anchor is that BLS/FRED federal payroll employment was already down about 97,000 from the January 2025 peak by August. On top of that, multiple pre-cutoff reports indicated a large deferred-resignation/buyout cohort—over 150,000 accepted offers and many remained on paid administrative leave until Sept. 30—so a sizable October/November step-down in the payroll count looked plausible. If most of that cohort actually rolled off CEU9091000001, the market only needed a modest additional net decline to clear -250,000 by year-end. The main reason this is not much higher is measurement mismatch: OPM/Reuters/agency ‘departures’ are not the same as the BLS/FRED payroll series, some cuts were slowed by courts, some agencies were rehiring, and the official September/October data were delayed by the shutdown. Netting those together, YES looked somewhat more likely than NO, but only narrowly.

As of 2025-11-09, Musk has clearly made the public-announcement half of the resolution, but the stronger evidence points to the required FEC/legal-formation half still not having happened. Reuters reported Musk said on July 5 that 'the America Party is formed,' but multiple mainstream outlets soon after said there was no genuine FEC registration, and Musk himself called the circulating filing false. Later July/August coverage still described the effort as lacking visible formal steps or official filings. With less than two months left in the year and no clear sign of renewed organizational push, a late authentic filing is possible but unlikely.

By Nov. 9, the key event needed for YES appears very likely to have already happened. Multiple major outlets reported arrests on Oct. 26, and later reporting described the first two detainees as suspects who police say directly participated in the heist, with partial admissions and DNA links to the scene (search_0_result_9, search_1_result_9, search_1_result_0, search_3_result_2). Reuters follow-up also implied that arrested suspects were among the physical perpetrators, with only one organiser still on the run and, by early November, three of the four suspected thieves believed to be under arrest (search_2_result_1, search_3_result_0). The main residual risk is market-definition risk: some later detainees were released or denied involvement, so there remains a chance the early arrests are later undermined or reclassified. But given the volume and specificity of reporting, YES is the clear favorite.

By 2025-11-09, AI use in Hollywood was clearly rising, but almost all concrete reporting pointed to hidden/incremental use, not a publicly acknowledged $100M+ theatrical release whose script was substantially AI-written or AI-assisted. Trade coverage emphasized stigma, copyright and labor constraints, and even pro-AI voices described the first real AI feature as still in the future. With less than two months left in the resolution window and no identified qualifying candidate, YES looked unlikely but not impossible.

Late-2025 frontier models look impressive and increasingly agentic, but the strongest evidence still falls short of a clear, consensus case that an AGI 'as smart as a generally educated human' already exists. Primary-source benchmarks show meaningful progress, yet not robust human-level reliability across hard real-world tasks; major lab leaders still describe important holes; and the question resolves via a subjective poll, where participants are likely to apply a stricter standard than 'sometimes benchmark-comparable.' There is some chance that late-2025 releases shift opinion, but from the Nov. 9 vantage point that looks unlikely to be enough for a YES majority.

Virgin Galactic looked financially distressed but not on the verge of a bankruptcy filing before 2026. The most important fact is liquidity: the company still reported more than $500 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of June 30, 2025, while guiding to roughly $100M-$110M of negative free cash flow for Q3 2025 (search_1_summary). Even assuming continued heavy burn, that suggests material runway remained into 2026 rather than an immediate cash wall in Nov-Dec 2025. Earlier data are consistent with this gradual drawdown rather than sudden insolvency: $744M cash in Q3 2024 (search_0_summary) and $1.1B in mid-2022 (lookup_1). On top of cash, the company had announced a $300M at-the-market equity program in Nov. 2024, giving it at least some continued financing flexibility (search_3_summary). 

The case for bankruptcy is real but weaker over this short horizon: commercial operations were paused, revenue was negligible, Delta development kept consuming cash, and the business remained dependent on outside financing or its balance sheet rather than operating cash generation (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). If there were a hidden covenant breach, debt maturity, or abrupt financing freeze, bankruptcy risk would be higher. But I did not find authoritative evidence before the cutoff of an imminent maturity or going-concern event that would likely force an actual filing before Jan. 1, 2026. Because the horizon is only about seven weeks from the cutoff, absence of an immediate trigger matters a lot. So I put the chance modestly below the market estimate, at 11%.

China clearly wants more overseas military access, and BRICS expansion gives it more plausible hosts, especially the UAE, Egypt, Ethiopia, and South Africa. But the strongest pre-cutoff official evidence still says Djibouti is the only current overseas Chinese base, while the BRICS-country sites are described as considered or prospective logistics facilities rather than operating bases. The most plausible YES path is a late-2025 US intelligence disclosure about the UAE/Khalifa Port site, but the public evidence for that is dated and ambiguous, and there is very little time left before 2026. So YES is possible but still unlikely.

Very unlikely. The outside view is brutal: Swiss Federal Tribunal challenges to CAS awards succeed only on narrow procedural grounds and historically at well under 1% rates. In this case, Chiles did file a real appeal and raised cognizable grounds (right to be heard / panel-constitution issues), plus there is video evidence that would matter if a court or remanded CAS panel considered it. But CAS itself refused to reopen, the medal was already reallocated, Swiss review does not normally revisit the underlying factual timing dispute, and as of August 2025 the appeal was still pending with no clear decision timeline. With less than five months from that report to Jan. 1, 2026, the combination of low legal success rate and short remaining horizon makes an overturn before 2026 only about 5%.

I estimate a 5% chance. By May 2025, CDC-reviewed H5N1 viruses from California and Washington still had only moderate emergence scores of 5.59 and 5.21, below the earlier Texas H5N1 score of 5.79 and far below the 8.0 'high' threshold (search_0_result_0). Mid-2025 authoritative reporting still indicated no confirmed human-to-human transmission in the US H5N1 outbreak (search_2_result_1), and Reuters reported in July 2025 that CDC still considered the current public health risk from H5N1 to be low (search_6_result_5). Given the short remaining window from the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff to Jan. 1, 2026, plus the need for a specific CDC IRAT publication with a very large upward revision, YES looks very unlikely, though not impossible if late-breaking evidence forced a rapid reassessment.

Franchise films already had a strong base rate to finish #1 worldwide, and the 2025 inside view makes the case even stronger. By mid/late 2025, 'Ne Zha 2'—a clear sequel/franchise film—was widely reported as the year's top grosser at roughly $2.1B-$2.2B, and the main identified challenger was 'Avatar: Fire and Ash,' which is also a franchise entry. For NO to occur, an implausible late non-franchise film would have to beat one of the largest grosses in movie history, so YES is overwhelmingly likely.

Rockstar officially announced on May 2, 2025 that Grand Theft Auto VI was moved from fall 2025 to May 26, 2026. Given the resolution criterion requires the game to be available for sale in Europe during calendar year 2025, that official delay makes a YES outcome extremely unlikely. A YES would require Rockstar to reverse its own delay and launch roughly six months earlier than the announced 2026 date before Nov. 30, 2025, which is possible in principle but very implausible.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the key uncertainty is mostly resolved in direction if not in the exact final Eurostat line item. Spain was already far ahead in Q3 2024 (+3.5% vs euro area +0.9% per the question background), and late-October 2025 reporting shows Spain still outperforming in Q3 2025 on quarterly growth (about 0.6% vs 0.2% for the euro area). A contemporaneous article citing Eurostat gives euro area annual GDP growth in Q3 2025 at about 1.5%, while Reuters reports Spain remained the euro zone’s fastest-growing large economy and cites 2025 Spain growth forecasts around 2.7%-2.9%. If Spain’s Q3 2025 y/y figure is anywhere near those levels, the gap over the euro area is large enough that normal revisions are very unlikely to erase it. The main residual risk is that I did not find the exact official Eurostat Spain y/y Q3 2025 number before submitting, but the available evidence strongly favors YES.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the main realistic vehicle for major tax-code revisions—the July 4 reconciliation law (OBBBA / P.L. 119-21)—had already been enacted. Multiple post-enactment legal analyses describe 45Y domestic content as still an optional bonus/adder, with OBBBA merely adjusting thresholds for projects seeking that adder, not converting it into a universal eligibility requirement (search_0_result_1) (search_0_result_2) (search_0_result_3) (search_0_result_5). That makes it unlikely the question had already effectively resolved YES through the big bill. The remaining path was a separate late-2025 enacted bill carrying this very specific amendment to §45Y, which looks unlikely given the absence of evidence for an active vehicle and the general fact that the major reconciliation/tax package had already passed (lookup_1) (search_2_result_3).

I put this below 20%. The case has real cert-worthy features—a consequential federal preemption issue, a claimed circuit split, Chamber support, and most importantly a June 30, 2025 CVSG, which shows some Supreme Court interest. But this question asks about a grant by January 15, 2026, not eventual review. As of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the available evidence did not show the SG had already filed or that the Court had relisted/held/granted the petition; after a CVSG, timing often becomes the main bottleneck. Combined with Durnell’s possible vehicle problems and the Court’s earlier reluctance in related Roundup litigation, the most likely outcome by Jan. 15 looked like the petition still being pending or being denied, not granted.

Russia conducting an actual nuclear explosive test in the remaining weeks of 2025 is still unlikely, because such tests are extraordinarily rare, Russia has not done one since 1990, and the diplomatic/escalatory costs of crossing the threshold are very high. But the risk is materially above a pure base rate because in early November Putin ordered proposals on possible resumed testing and Belousov said Novaya Zemlya could host tests on short notice. The main reason I stay in the low teens rather than higher is that Russia's public line has remained conditional on a U.S. test first, and as of Nov. 4-7 Moscow was still seeking clarification of U.S. intentions, which looks more like coercive signaling and contingency planning than a committed near-term decision. I also have not found authoritative evidence of explosive-test-specific preparations or monitoring alarms that would indicate an imminent detonation before Jan. 1, 2026.

Bitcoin had strong upside momentum earlier in Q4 2025, reaching a fresh ATH around $125.8k in early October, so a $170k touch was not absurd on an outside view. But by the Nov. 9 cutoff, the more relevant evidence suggests BTC had retraced to roughly $103k-$110k amid trade-war/government-shutdown/macro stress and weaker ETF flow momentum. From that level, $170k requires roughly a 55-65% rally in under two months. BTC can make very large moves, and the resolution only needs a one-minute Binance wick, which slightly raises the odds versus requiring a sustained close. Still, for an already very large asset, a further ~60% surge over that horizon after a sharp retracement looks like a tail event rather than a central case.

Hamas had moved from categorical silence toward limited discussion of partial weapons decommissioning by early November, which keeps a YES outcome alive, especially because staged or partial disarmament would qualify. But the stronger and more recent evidence still points the other way: senior Hamas figures would not commit to disarmament, Reuters continued to report on Nov. 5 that Hamas had not agreed to disarm/demilitarize Gaza, and the group’s identity and bargaining posture favor ambiguity and conditions rather than an official public commitment. With only about seven weeks remaining from the Nov. 9 forecast date, I see a formal qualifying announcement as possible but clearly unlikely.

By the Nov. 9 cutoff, the Fed had delivered only two 25 bp cuts in 2025: the first on Sept. 17 to 4.00%-4.25%, and the second on Oct. 29 to 3.75%-4.00% (search_1_summary). There was only one scheduled FOMC meeting left, on Dec. 10; no November meeting remained (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_1, search_2_result_5, search_2_result_6). So for YES to resolve, the Fed would need at least 150 bp more easing in about a month—effectively six additional 25 bp cuts—through the December meeting and/or emergency intermeeting action. That is a crisis-scale move. But late-October communications pointed the other way: Powell said a December cut was not assured, inflation was near 3%, and markets priced only about a two-thirds chance of any December cut at all, centered on a normal 25 bp step rather than a jumbo move (search_1_summary, search_3_result_0, search_3_result_4, search_3_result_6, search_3_result_9). There was some evidence of labor-market softening and an easing bias, but nothing close to what would normally precede 150+ bp of cuts in one month. So YES looks like only a remote tail-risk scenario.

A 50+ bp cut at the January 2026 FOMC meeting looked possible but distinctly unlikely as of 2025-11-09. The outside view is already low: scheduled 50 bp Fed cuts are rare and usually require recessionary or financial-stress conditions. By the cutoff, the Fed had shifted into easing mode because the labor market was softening, but the baseline from both policymakers and markets still pointed to quarter-point moves, not a half-point January step. Reuters polling in late October expected 25 bp cuts in November and December, the September dot plot implied only one 25 bp cut in all of 2026, and FedWatch-style pricing emphasized steady 25 bp increments. At the same time, inflation was still above target and growth was still around potential rather than collapsing. So the main path to YES required a material downside shock between Nov. 9 and the Jan. 28 meeting—possible, but not the central scenario.

Bournazel is a real candidate with Renaissance/Horizons backing, but as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff he looks clearly behind. Multiple reports put him around 14-15% in first-round polling, versus roughly 26-28% for Rachida Dati and about 20-22% for Emmanuel Grégoire. He also suffers from low name recognition, and the available second-round discussion makes him look more like a spoiler or bargaining chip than the most likely eventual mayor. His main upside path would be a Dati collapse or unusual alliance/council dynamics, but the strongest evidence before the cutoff does not show that happening. So his chance looks lower than the 11% market price, but still above zero because Paris politics is fragmented and indirect.

As of 2025-11-09, the balance of evidence favored Trump making a 2025 announcement rather than refraining. Recent Reuters/CNBC reporting showed an active shortlist process, Bessent planning to present finalists after Thanksgiving, and Trump saying he anticipated naming Powell’s replacement by year-end. That said, the timeline was still tentative, compressed into December, and there was a natural option to wait for an early-2026 Fed board vacancy before formalizing the move. So 'no announcement by Dec. 31, 2025' remained a substantial minority outcome, but not the most likely one.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the album had clearly debuted at No. 1 and already logged four straight weeks, with a still-large week-4 lead over No. 2 and a relatively light late-2025 release calendar. That makes the chance materially higher than the generic base rate. But 18 straight weeks is an extremely high bar: search results indicate the longest Billboard 200 run from debut is 13 weeks, and Swift’s own previous blockbuster opened with 12 straight weeks but still fell short of 18 consecutive. Since Showgirl’s post-debut pace appears weaker than TTPD by week 2, and it would need to stay uninterrupted into mid-February 2026, I think YES is still a clear long shot, though not as tiny as 1%.

I put the chance of no change after the March 2026 FOMC meeting at 61%. Outside view: most individual FOMC meetings are holds. Inside view: as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the Fed had just cut in October 2025 to 3.75%-4.00%, so the easing cycle was still active, which lowers the hold probability from a generic meeting baseline. But Powell also said the October cut may be the last of 2025 and that a December cut was not assured, while the FOMC’s September dot plot showed only one cut in all of 2026. That combination suggests March 2026 is slightly more likely to be a pause than an action meeting. Offsetting that, Reuters/CME-related reporting said markets were pricing multiple 2026 cuts and economists broadly expected another December 2025 cut, so a March move remained a substantial possibility. Netting those forces out, 'no change' looks a bit more likely than not, but far from certain.

Article 5 invocation remained a low-probability tail event as of Nov. 9, 2025. The outside view is overwhelmingly negative: NATO has invoked Article 5 only once ever. The inside view did raise risk somewhat because 2025 saw repeated Russian-linked drone incursions, airspace violations, and hybrid attacks against NATO members. But the strongest concrete incidents in September led affected allies to invoke Article 4 consultations, not Article 5, and NATO’s response was to reinforce defenses rather than declare collective self-defense. Reuters reporting through late October—and still on Nov. 5—described persistent hybrid threats and eastern-flank reinforcement, not an Article 5 move. With most of the market window elapsed and no invocation by the Nov. 9 cutoff, only a sudden major overt attack in the remaining weeks could flip this to YES. Because the allowed range bottoms out at 5%, I submit 5%.

OpenAI could still make a surprise rhetorical/self-serving declaration, but as of 2025-11-09 the odds look very low. The strongest pro-YES evidence is Altman’s January statement that OpenAI is confident it knows how to build AGI. However, newer and more authoritative late-2025 evidence points the other way: OpenAI/Altman are de-emphasizing the term AGI in favor of 'personal AGI,' 'superintelligence,' and a gradual 'gentle singularity'; OpenAI’s revised Microsoft agreement preserves formal consequences around AGI and requires independent panel verification of AGI claims; and even amid major October 2025 launches and restructuring news, OpenAI still had not declared AGI. With only ~7 weeks left after the cutoff, this looks like roughly a 1-in-20 event.

I would price this below 5% absent the floor, but under the allowed range I submit 5%. By Nov. 9, 2025, the February HKU5-CoV-2 story still appears to have been a laboratory/animal-virus finding with no confirmed human cases or sustained transmission (search_1_result_1, search_1_result_2, search_1_result_3). WHO’s 2025 materials surface routine COVID and sporadic MERS items, plus long-term coronavirus threat-management planning, not response to a new coronavirus emergency (search_3_result_1, search_3_summary). On top of that, WHO does not have a formal pandemic declaration category and often uses PHEIC/emergency language instead, which makes the exact resolution trigger even harder to hit (search_5_result_0, search_5_result_2). With less than two months left in the year and no sign of an internationally visible new-human-coronavirus outbreak, YES looks very unlikely.

By 2025-11-09, the best independent evidence pointed strongly against a sub-1.10°C NASA GISTEMP October 2025 anomaly. Recent Octobers in NASA were extremely warm, with October 2024 second only to October 2023 and September 2024 already above 1.10°C on NASA’s 1951-1980 baseline. Most importantly, Copernicus reported October 2025 as the third-warmest October on record and 2025 as on track to be among the three warmest years. Weak late-2025 La Niña odds provide only modest cooling and are unlikely to drag NASA below 1.10°C unless NASA diverges unusually sharply from other datasets. So YES is possible, but only at the floor of my plausible range.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, there was strong pre-cutoff evidence that the event had already occurred: multiple mainstream outlets reported that, according to the Bloomberg Billionaires Index, Larry Ellison briefly surpassed Elon Musk on Sept. 10, 2025, with Ellison around $393B vs Musk around $385B, and one source indicated Ellison was ahead for much of the day before Musk retook #1 the next morning. Since the market resolves YES if Musk is anything other than #1 at any point between June 30 and Dec. 31, this looks very likely to have already satisfied the criterion. I stop short of 95%+ certainty only because I have not directly inspected Bloomberg’s own archived index snapshot, leaving a small residual risk of media paraphrase or resolution-source ambiguity.

Moonshot is a real contender because Kimi K2 reportedly reached #1 on LMArena in July 2025, and Moonshot launched K2 Thinking on Nov. 6 with strong claimed frontier benchmarks. But the more decision-relevant evidence near the cutoff is weaker: by September Kimi K2 had fallen to a tie for 8th on LMArena text, while major incumbents like Google and then Anthropic were reported at #1 in September/October. Arena leadership can change quickly, so a late-year rebound is plausible, but Moonshot would need both a successful Arena climb from outside the top tier and then to hold off OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/xAI/Qwen through Dec. 31. That makes Moonshot a live but clearly secondary contender, around 13%.

October 2025 looked too warm for this narrow NASA bin. Copernicus reported October 2025 as the third-warmest October on record and 1.55°C above 1850-1900 (search_4_result_0), which usually maps to a NASA GISTEMP monthly anomaly comfortably above 1.14°C once baseline differences are accounted for. Cross-dataset comparisons for 2024 suggest NASA tends to run only a few hundredths below the highest estimates, not ~0.15-0.20°C lower (search_5_result_1). 2025 did cool somewhat versus 2024, so a small tail chance remains, but 1.10-1.14°C seems materially too low.

Google’s Year in Search ranks fastest-growing people searches, not raw fame, which hurts Biden because he was already a top-five global trending person in 2024. He did get a real 2025 spike from his prostate cancer diagnosis, but the field looks crowded with fresher, likely larger global surges—especially Pope Francis’ death, Pope Leo XIV’s election, and other viral figures like Bianca Censori. So Biden being exactly #2 looks unlikely; my estimate is at the 5% floor.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Crystal Palace were 12th with 14 points from 11 matches, a -1 goal difference, and 15 points behind Arsenal at the top (lookup_0). That is an extremely poor title position for any club, especially one whose outside-view base rate is already tiny. Supporting this, late-September odds were still only 66/1 (~1.5% implied) even after a strong run, and major November projections had Palace finishing 9th rather than in the title race (search_1_summary, search_0_summary). Their FA Cup/Community Shield wins and unbeaten stretch show they were a decent side, but not enough to overcome a historically bad title outlook from 12th place. I therefore use the minimum allowed forecast: 5%, with the true estimate likely lower.

By the cutoff, Bitcoin was still trading around $101.5k in early November after making all-time highs above $125k in early October. Reaching $50k by Dec. 31 would therefore require roughly a 50% additional collapse in about seven weeks, far below the known 2025 lows. That kind of move is possible in crypto, especially since resolution is on any 1-minute Binance low, but it would likely require an exchange/systemic shock or an unusually severe macro panic. The October liquidation event and earlier 2025 selloffs show nontrivial tail risk, yet available evidence still points to low-single-digit odds rather than a central scenario. The 0.04 market price from Oct. 30 also looks broadly consistent; I’m slightly above it because a brief wick counts.

Mistral is a real but small contender. The strongest pro-YES evidence is that Mistral Medium 3.1 was reportedly very close to the frontier in late August 2025—#1 in English and #2 overall on LM Arena in one reported view—so this is not a pure long shot. But the stronger overall picture is still negative: Arena leadership is highly volatile; larger rivals like Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and DeepSeek were all actively shipping new models through 2025; and I found no solid evidence by the Nov. 9 cutoff of a newer Mistral flagship after Medium 3.1 that was poised to take or hold the overall top spot by Dec. 31. Mistral also loses important alphabetical tie-breaks against several major rivals. Net: plausible, but clearly an underdog.

By Nov. 9, a naive exact-position base rate of ~10% had to be cut sharply by the actual late-season standings context. Official F1 coverage before Sao Paulo had Williams in a 'firm grip' on P5, while Racing Bulls were 6th on 72 points and grouped tightly with Aston Martin, Haas, and Sauber. After Sao Paulo, Racing Bulls did score well and likely narrowed the gap a bit, but reporting still framed them as part of that 6th-9th midfield pack rather than as the main challenger for 5th. With three races and one sprint still left, a swing to P5 remained possible, but it looked like a clear longshot rather than a live coinflip. I therefore put YES at 5%.

I put Foxen at the 5% floor. Ex ante, even an elite player in a 64-player single-elimination heads-up bracket only has low-single-digit title odds, and Foxen also drew very tough first-round opposition in Jesse Lonis. More importantly, by Nov. 9 the tournament had already been played in August, CardPlayer had a finalized results page up, a spoiler article reported that the winner had leaked before broadcast, and the prompt notes the market was already at 0.00 on Oct. 30. That combination makes it very likely Foxen was not the winner; I would go below 5% if allowed, but the forecasting rules impose a floor.

Zootopia 2 had a credible path on Nov. 9: it had a prime Nov. 26 Thanksgiving launch, strong tracking, and a favorable family-animation lane. But the hurdle was already high—A Minecraft Movie appeared to be the domestic leader at about $423.9M—and only 36 days of Zootopia 2's run would count. Trade/forecast signals around the cutoff suggested Zootopia 2 was more of a borderline-than-likely $424M+ calendar-gross candidate: Boxoffice Pro had it at $95M-$110M 3-day / $140M-$160M 5-day (search_5_result_1), another forecast range put its domestic total at roughly $394M-$486M while giving Wicked: For Good an even higher $463M-$519M total range (search_5_result_4), and Cinelytic commentary treated merely crossing $400M domestic as a strong outcome (search_5_result_8). Since calendar gross by Dec. 31 will usually be below total domestic gross for a Nov. 26 release, Zootopia 2 likely needed a top-end run plus some rival underperformance. Overall that made YES plausible but distinctly less likely than NO.

By the cutoff, the strongest evidence says Starship had only five 2025 launches through Flight 11 on Oct. 13, and likely five qualifying space-reaching launches under the market’s 62-mile rule. From that point, hitting the 10-12 band would require 5-7 additional successful launches in the remaining weeks of 2025—far faster than the observed cadence, especially with Flight 12 involving a transition to Pad 2/Block 3 and reports that there was still substantial work before it could fly. FAA launch-cap approval kept the door open in theory, but actual execution lagged badly. So YES looked essentially out of reach, but not impossible, hence 5%.

Indiana had a credible preseason case as one of the Central Division’s top three teams, but the cutoff-date evidence is overwhelmingly negative. The Pacers were reported by AP to have started 0-5 and, after a first win, then lost to Milwaukee and Brooklyn, leaving them 1-7 by Nov. 6. In a division with strong rivals like Cleveland and Milwaukee, that kind of hole makes a division title very unlikely. I leave a small residual chance because Indiana had recent finalist/East-champion pedigree and the exact rival records/injury context are still somewhat uncertain, but 5% is my best estimate.

A U.S. territorial invasion of Iran by Dec. 31, 2025 is far less likely than limited strikes. By the Nov. 9 cutoff, the actual 2025 escalation had taken the form of U.S. airstrikes on nuclear sites and then de-escalation/containment, not a ground campaign. Reuters also reported the Pentagon was scaling back its Iraq mission in October, which is inconsistent with preparing for an invasion of neighboring Iran. The main residual risk is a low-probability tail event in which a fresh crisis after Nov. 9 triggers a rapid, limited seizure of Iranian territory, but that seems unlikely.

I estimate a 5% chance. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the public record points strongly to an air/naval campaign plus possible support for local anti-Houthi forces, not overt deployment of regular U.S. ground troops into Yemen. Pentagon officials explicitly said in March they did not have ground troops in Yemen and were not sending them, and later reporting described a May U.S.-Houthi ceasefire that reduced escalation pressure. With less than two months left to resolve and no credible reporting of qualifying entry so far, only tail-risk contingencies remain—such as a sudden late-year re-escalation or emergency recovery mission by regular troops.

A 64-player single-elimination heads-up event gives a field-average chance of about 1.6%. Gierse was definitely in the bracket and looks stronger than average thanks to elite online heads-up/tournament credentials, so his true ex ante chance should be above average. But the field was loaded with top pros, the format is very high variance, and by the cutoff there were already spoiler/result references without Gierse surfacing as the obvious leaked winner. Net: still a live contender, but only a long shot.

Google looks favored because Gemini 2.5 Pro had a sizable August lead on Chatbot Arena and, crucially, a Nov. 2 source still said it was beating GPT-5 and Claude 4.5 in LMArena. With only ~7 weeks left from the cutoff, incumbency at #1 matters a lot. The main reason this is not higher is that Arena rankings can change quickly and xAI had publicly teased Grok 5 before year-end, so a late disruptive release remained plausible.

By Nov. 9, 2025, there is no sign WHO has already declared any disease a pandemic in 2025, and the main plausible candidates are not close to that threshold. WHO’s own materials emphasize that a PHEIC is its main formal alarm and that even for COVID-19 it used 'pandemic' later as descriptive language, which suggests such declarations are rare. The strongest candidate, H5N1, was still assessed as low global public-health risk in late July 2025, and mpox was being managed through emergency-committee/PHEIC channels rather than pandemic language, with late-2025 overviews discussing many outbreaks but no WHO pandemic declaration. With only about seven weeks left in the year, the remaining hazard is very small, though not zero because a rapid deterioration in a respiratory pathogen could still force WHO to change its language.

I estimate a 7% chance that Friedrich Merz ceases to be Chancellor of Germany at any point from Nov. 9 through Dec. 31, 2025. The outside view is low for a newly installed German chancellor to leave office within just the final ~7 weeks of the year. Merz is politically weaker than normal—his coalition has shown indiscipline, trust frictions, and he had very low approval by October—so this is not near zero. But the more current and authoritative evidence points away from an imminent exit: Reuters still described him as chancellor on Oct. 20 while saying coalition leaders had a good working relationship, and the federal government was still issuing statements by Merz as chancellor on Oct. 23. I found no clear pre-cutoff reporting of an announced resignation, removal, or impending no-confidence mechanism. So the risk is real but small, and mostly concentrated in a surprise coalition rupture, scandal, or health event.

I estimate a 6% chance. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Iran clearly retained dangerous latent capability: Reuters and IAEA reporting said Iran had amassed over 400 kg of 60%-enriched uranium, very near weapons grade (search_0_result_0), and some reporting cited Israeli claims of progress on weapon-related components before the June strikes (search_4_result_8). That keeps a covert sprint to a bomb within the realm of possibility.

But the resolution bar is higher than capability: it requires credible confirmation that Iran possesses a nuclear weapon by Dec. 31. The strongest authoritative signals I found cut against that. Mid-2025 IAEA and U.S. intelligence reporting still said there was no evidence Iran was building a weapon (search_1_result_1; search_1_result_6). After the June war, the IAEA said it had lost continuity of knowledge over key aspects of Iran's program (search_2_result_6), and the September deal to resume inspections was reportedly voided by Iran in October (search_3_result_0). So even if weaponization risk rose, credible confirmation by year-end became harder.

With only about seven weeks remaining from the cutoff, and no late-October/early-November authoritative report that Iran had decided to build or already possessed a bomb, the event looks unlikely but not impossible.

Meta looks like a long shot. The strongest public evidence before the cutoff is that Meta’s vanilla Llama 4 Maverick was not top-tier on Chatbot Arena relative to OpenAI/Anthropic/Google, and LM Arena tightened policies after the optimized-Maverick controversy, which reduces the odds that Meta can climb via a benchmark-tuned special variant. Reuters also reported Behemoth was delayed because it was not improving enough, weakening the most plausible path to a late-2025 leap. There is some upside from reporting that Meta was aiming to launch a new Llama 4.X model by year-end, but as of October reporting the latest released Llama was still the April 2025 generation, implying no clear flagship breakthrough had arrived by the cutoff. With only ~7 weeks to resolution and several rivals already at or near the frontier, I put Meta at the floor-ish end of plausible probabilities.

Washington looked like an extreme longshot by the 2025-11-09 cutoff. They were 3-6, on a four-game losing streak, outside the playoff picture, and ranked 13th in the NFC (search_0_summary, search_2_summary). Their starting QB Jayden Daniels had suffered a dislocated elbow and was expected to miss several games, forcing Marcus Mariota into the lineup (search_1_summary). Independent probability signals were also very low: ESPN reportedly had them at just a 2% chance to make the Super Bowl, and BetMGM had them at +8000 to win it while projecting them to miss the postseason (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). The only meaningful positive is that they had recently reached the NFC title game and still had a talented young QB core (search_0_summary). Net: true odds look in the low single digits or lower, so under the platform's probability floor I submit 5%.

Fourteen straight weeks at No. 1 is rare, so the outside view starts low. But this is not a normal album case: 'The Life of a Showgirl' is a Taylor Swift release, it debuted with a historic 4.002 million units, and Billboard had it still at No. 1 through four straight weeks as of the Nov. 8 chart. Swift’s previous studio album also managed a 17-week No. 1 run, and one trade report suggested there were no obvious late-2025 pop blockbusters queued up to stop the streak immediately. Still, the album’s weekly units have already decayed sharply to 146k by week 4, its lead over No. 2 was down to about 62k, and it would still need roughly 10 more uninterrupted weekly wins through mid-January 2026. That long remaining path leaves plenty of cumulative upset risk from a strong new release, holiday-season dynamics, or a faster-than-expected decay curve. Net: a real contender, but still clearly below even odds.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the strongest evidence suggests BTC had already peaked above $125k in early October but then retraced to roughly $100-103k by early November. From that level, reaching $140k by Dec. 31 would require about a 35-38% rally in ~7 weeks. That is definitely possible for Bitcoin, especially since resolution only needs a Binance 1-minute high, not a close. But the latest pre-cutoff evidence points to weakening momentum: a Reuters report had BTC down to $104,782 by Oct. 12 amid trade-war pressure, CoinDesk had it near $103k on Nov. 4, and other Nov. 7-9 reports placed it around $101-103k with recent ETF outflows. So I see this as a meaningful but sub-25% chance.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Burden looks like a fringe long shot rather than a real OROY contender. He is a second-round WR in a crowded Bears offense, had only one early splash game, and by Week 10 had just 13 catches for 171 yards and 1 TD. Midseason rookie coverage focused on players like Jaxson Dart, Emeka Egbuka, Ashton Jeanty, and Cam Ward—not Burden. He still has some tail risk if he explodes late and the leaders fade, but from this information set his chances are near the floor.

Sentimental Value appears to be a real Best Picture contender, not a fringe longshot: it has elite festival/critical reception, an awards-friendly release and campaign from Neon, and multiple major pundit lists placing it near the top of the field. But as of 2025-11-09, the strongest race narratives still center on Hamnet and One Battle After Another, with Sentimental Value usually running just behind them. So it looks more likely to be nominated than to actually win, leaving it in the low-teens rather than as a frontrunner.

By 2025-11-09, the key fact is that A Minecraft Movie had already set a very high 2025 opening-weekend benchmark at about $162.7M. Wicked: For Good had strong presales and some bullish early tracking, but the most recent trade estimate I found (Deadline, Oct. 31) was only $112M-$115M for the 3-day opening, and even the more bullish Boxoffice Pro range of $130M-$155M still sat mostly below the number needed. Remaining late-year competitors like Zootopia 2 and Avatar: Fire and Ash looked more likely to open below Wicked than above it, so the main path to YES is a major upside surprise by Wicked itself. That seems possible, but still clearly an underdog outcome.

This looks like a long shot. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, available awards coverage for 'Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere' focused on Best Picture/acting/screenplay rather than Production Design, and the film is repeatedly characterized as an intimate, stark, lo-fi 1982-set biopic rather than a major design-showcase contender. There is some positive evidence that the film’s period recreation is authentic and that at least one review noticed the production design, but not enough to offset the apparent lack of category-specific momentum. Since failure to be nominated resolves NO immediately, the biggest obstacle is even reaching the nomination slate. I therefore put it at the floor of the allowed range.

Ryan Feldman’s chance was already very low on outside-view grounds: this is a 64-player single-elimination invitational, so an average entrant is only about 1.56% to win, and Feldman had a brutal round-one draw against Sean Winter. By the Nov. 9 cutoff, multiple pre-cutoff reports suggested the event had effectively been spoiled via prediction-market activity, with Sam Soverel trading as an overwhelming favorite/presumptive winner. That makes a Feldman win highly unlikely, leaving only residual uncertainty about the leak/reporting accuracy.

DeepSeek looks like a real frontier contender, but the pre-cutoff evidence points to it being competitive rather than the overall Chatbot Arena leader. Gemini 2.5 Pro was reported #1 in May, and by August GPT-5 was reported atop LM Arena, with late-2025 coverage focusing mainly on OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google as the top-tier overall leaders. DeepSeek appears strongest as an open/open-weight contender, not the incumbent overall #1. For YES to resolve, DeepSeek likely needs a major surprise release between Nov. 9 and Dec. 31 that leapfrogs several elite rivals, while those rivals fail to answer. That is possible but unlikely over such a short horizon. I therefore put this near the floor at 5%.

OpenAI is a real contender, but the best available pre-cutoff evidence suggests it was not leading Chatbot Arena in early November 2025: Gemini 2.5 Pro was reportedly ahead of GPT-5 and Claude 4.5. OpenAI’s main plausible path is an iterative GPT-5.x improvement, because GPT-6 was explicitly not shipping in 2025. That keeps some chance alive, especially since GPT-5 reportedly led Arena in August, but the short ~7-week horizon, Google’s apparent existing lead, likely continued competitor releases (including Gemini 3 targeted for 2025 and Claude 4.5 already out), and unfavorable alphabetical tie-breaks versus Google/Anthropic all push the probability down. Net: OpenAI has a nontrivial but clearly minority chance to hold the top spot on Dec. 31.

By Nov. 9, 2025, the evidence overwhelmingly pointed to A11pl3Z/3I-ATLAS being a normal interstellar comet, not alien life or technology. NASA sources described a solid icy nucleus, dust, a gaseous coma, and a CO2-rich coma, while the Pentagon/AARO reportedly assessed it as a comet outside UAP channels. Since the market resolves on a definitive statement by a qualifying US official, the remaining YES path is mostly a low-probability political or communications surprise rather than a science-driven shift.

By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, the Fed had only cut twice in 2025: September to 4.00%-4.25% and October to 3.75%-4.00%, after holding steady through July. That means this market can resolve YES only if the December meeting delivers a 50bp cut, which counts as two 25bp cuts. But late-October/early-November coverage and market pricing were focused on a standard 25bp December cut versus no cut, not 50bp. One October dissent favored 50bp, so YES is not impossible, but it looks like a low-probability tail outcome.

Portland was still alive as of 2025-11-09 and had helped itself by opening NBA Cup play with a 109-107 win over Denver, but it still looked like a long shot overall. Preseason expectations were weak, West Group C was widely seen as the toughest group, and by Nov. 8 Portland was not clearly favored even within the group or for the West wild card. So this is a low-single-digit chance rounded to 5%.

This looks like a floor-level YES. The outside view is extremely unfavorable: US allies almost never newly acquire independently controlled nuclear weapons on a sub-year timescale, and by Nov. 9 there was still no credible reporting of a qualifying ally actually possessing an operational weapon. The main candidates all looked weak. South Korean nuclear talk was real, but Reuters reported the new Lee Jae Myung administration rejected armament by August, while Japan’s debate centered on nuclear sharing, which would not qualify (search_1_result_2). European discussion in Poland and Germany was mostly about deterrence, sharing, or signaling; expert sources emphasized that countries like Japan, South Korea, and Poland cannot just decide to get nukes overnight, and called a 2025 ally bomb unlikely (search_0_result_5, search_0_result_8). Saudi Arabia had civil nuclear talks and a defense pact with Pakistan, but reporting characterized that as extended deterrence rather than transfer of independently controlled weapons (search_3_result_0, search_3_result_9). With only weeks left until Dec. 31 and no concrete evidence of testing, assembly, transfer, or official declaration, NO is overwhelmingly more likely.

I estimate an 18% chance of a qualifying NATO-Russia military encounter by March 31, 2026. The main upside case is accidental or semi-deliberate escalation from repeated Russian air incursions near NATO territory: Poland’s September 2025 shootdowns show NATO is now willing to use force against Russian-linked air objects, and Operation Eastern Sentry has increased the density of NATO aircraft on the eastern flank. But that September incident was before the market window, and the most authoritative reporting inside/near the window points the other way: late-October and early-November Reuters coverage describes interceptions, escorts, and hybrid pressure rather than exchanges of fire, while NATO’s top commander said Russia seems to have become more careful after earlier close calls. So the risk is materially above peacetime, but still clearly below one-in-three over the remaining horizon.

I put this near the floor. Outside-view, second-generation successors in brand-new Apple product categories are not usually on an annual cadence. More importantly, as of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff there was no official Apple hardware announcement or sales availability for a new Vision product in Apple Newsroom searches, only software updates for the existing headset (search_1_result_0, search_1_result_4). The most recent authoritative reporting had also shifted later: Bloomberg-linked coverage indicated Apple was aiming for Vision Pro 2 by spring 2026 rather than clearly in 2025 (search_2_summary), and Reuters reported Apple had halted a planned Vision Pro overhaul and shifted resources toward smart glasses (search_3_result_3). There is still a small residual chance of a quiet late-2025 chip-bump launch that would count as a successor, especially since some earlier rumor coverage still allowed for late 2025 (search_0_result_1, search_0_result_0). But with under two months left at the cutoff and no official release yet, NO is overwhelmingly more likely.

Philadelphia looks like a legitimate but not dominant contender as of 2025-11-09. The best outside-view anchor is that they entered 2025 as one of the top Super Bowl favorites, around +700 to +750 in August and 12% in ESPN FPI (search_3_result_1, search_3_result_7). More importantly, by Week 10 they were 6-2, in the NFC No. 1 seed race, and had added defensive reinforcements at the trade deadline, which supports a slightly higher chance than the 9% market price from 2025-10-30 (search_4_result_0, search_4_result_5). Offsetting that, they had shown some volatility with back-to-back losses in October and there were at least minor health concerns entering Week 10, including A.J. Brown managing a hamstring and Hurts missing a starting lineman (search_5_result_6, search_6_result_2, search_6_result_6). Netting this out, a low-teens probability is the most defensible estimate.

By 2025-11-09, the strongest affirmative evidence is still indirect: Michael Wolff claimed in May that the Trumps were effectively separated and living separate lives (search_0_result_1), and broader reporting suggested Melania was often away from the White House. But the more decision-relevant late evidence points the other way: the White House publicly denied Wolff’s claim (search_0_result_4), Reuters reported Melania making an official White House announcement on Oct. 10 (search_1_result_0), and Reuters photographed Donald and Melania hosting a White House Halloween event together on Oct. 30 (search_1_result_5). With no authoritative reports by Nov. 9 of legal filings, divorce negotiations, or a representative signaling an imminent split, and only about seven weeks left in the resolution window, a public divorce-intention announcement by year-end looks very unlikely. I therefore put this at the floor-ish low end: 5%.

I estimate a 5% chance. Musk had already served in a qualifying White House role, so a renewed appointment was procedurally possible. But the much more important evidence is that he left at the end of May, then almost immediately had a very public rupture with Trump in early June. By the Nov. 9 cutoff, repeated news and White House-site searches showed no sign of a real appointment process or official staffing announcement. There was a small residual chance because powerful political actors can reconcile quickly and White House adviser roles can be created fast, but by early November that chance looked close to the floor.

Stephen Chidwick is an elite player, but in a 64-player single-elimination heads-up event his ex-ante win chance is only a few percent. More importantly, by the forecast date there were multiple reports that the event had already been recorded in early August and that the winner had effectively leaked via prediction-market activity before broadcast. Combined with the prompt’s note that the market price for Chidwick was 0.00 on 2025-10-30, the most likely interpretation is that he had already been ruled out by informed traders. I could not independently verify the specific winner from pre-cutoff sources, so I won’t go all the way to certainty, but this looks very unlikely for Chidwick. Because of the probability floor, I submit 5%.

Major pre-cutoff reporting from Reuters, AP, BBC, and the Guardian indicates Israel launched large-scale strikes inside Iran on June 13, 2025, hitting Natanz and many other targets and killing senior commanders/scientists. That appears to match the stated resolution criteria: Israeli air/missile/drone strikes impacting Iranian soil before Dec. 31, 2025. The only meaningful residual doubt comes from the surprisingly low Oct. 30 market price, which suggests there may have been some hidden market-specific nuance or ambiguity not present in the prompt. On the prompt as written, though, YES looks overwhelmingly more likely than NO.

By early November 2025, the most authoritative late-year assessments were converging on 2025 finishing below 2024 rather than setting a new record. Copernicus said 2025 was 'virtually certain' to end as only the second- or third-warmest year, and October 2025 itself was cooler than October 2024, leaving only two months to erase the deficit. WMO/NOAA/Carbon Brief reporting also had 2025 pacing second or third, not first. The main reason this is not even lower is that the contract resolves on NASA GISTEMP specifically, and I do not have the exact NASA year-to-date deficit after October; a small cross-dataset difference could in principle matter. Still, given the broad late-year consensus and the shrinking runway, YES looks like only a small tail risk.

As of 2025-11-09, Highest 2 Lowest looks like a very unlikely winner. It is absent from credible early Best Makeup and Hairstyling contender lists, while Gold Derby’s craft-category predictions point to other films such as Frankenstein, Sinners, The Smashing Machine, and Wicked: For Good instead. Coverage of Highest 2 Lowest presents it as a contemporary New York crime drama with praise focused on Lee/Washington and style, not on conspicuous prosthetics, creature work, aging makeup, or transformative hairstyling—the kinds of elements that often drive this category. Since the film must first be nominated, and nomination itself already looks doubtful, I keep the forecast at the 5% floor.

By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, gold had already spent weeks far above the $3,200 threshold: Reuters reported breaks above $4,000 on Oct. 8, above $4,100 on Oct. 13, above $4,300 on Oct. 16, and Marex described an early-November trading range in the high $3,000s to mid-$4,000s. A Nov. 6 report also placed COMEX gold around $4,000. So for NO to occur, gold would likely need a roughly 20%+ drop in under two months. That is possible in a volatile commodity, and there were real bearish factors — a firmer dollar, easing trade tensions, and an 8% retreat from the October peak — but mainstream year-end expectations still sat above the threshold (UBS $3,800; WGC/forward curve roughly $3,300-$3,400). Overall, YES looks very likely but not certain.

Z.ai looks like a genuine contender, not a random long shot: searches show its GLM-4.5 launch was widely described as competitive with leading models, and one report says it ranked 3rd on a combined set of major benchmarks (search_1_result_0, search_1_result_5). But this market resolves specifically on Chatbot Arena, and despite several targeted searches I did not find direct evidence that a Z.ai model was already #1 or clearly near the top of that leaderboard by the Nov. 9 cutoff. Over only ~7 weeks, the top Arena slot is more likely to remain with one of the usual leading labs (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, DeepSeek) than to be captured by a company without clear current Arena leadership evidence. So I assign a small but real chance.

AICO looks overwhelmingly unlikely to win the most seats in the 2026 Colombian Senate election. The strongest evidence is structural: AICO won only 1 Senate seat in 2022 while the leading list had about 19 seats, and 2025 reporting suggests AICO is again acting as a small indigenous party selecting its own candidates rather than serving as the ballot label for a major national coalition. Reporting also indicates AICO mainly competes for the two reserved indigenous Senate seats, which makes finishing first overall extraordinarily implausible. Meanwhile, larger blocs such as Pacto Histórico and Alianza Verde/En Marcha are organizing separate Senate lists and are the more natural contenders for the most seats. I see no credible pre-cutoff evidence that AICO has national-scale polling, projections, or alliances that would put it anywhere near first place. Because of the platform’s minimum probability constraint, I submit 5%, though my unconstrained estimate would likely be lower.

BTC appears to have started the window far above the $90k barrier: Reuters reported an all-time high around $125,245 on Oct. 5 (search_1_result_0), and even during the major Oct. 10-11 selloff Reuters cited a low near $104,783 (search_4_result_7). A later early-November correction reportedly took BTC briefly below $100,000 but then back above $103,000, with support identified around $95k-$100k (search_1_result_7). Another Nov. 8 report put BTC around $102,311 (search_5_result_0). So, as of Nov. 9, the market likely had not yet hit $90k, and would still need roughly another 12% drop from about $102k to print $90k. That is very plausible for BTC over ~7 weeks, especially with Reuters noting investors were bracing for more volatility after October’s record liquidation event (search_4_result_3), but it is still less likely than not because BTC already absorbed multiple sharp drawdowns without reaching the barrier and had shown rebound/support in the upper-$90ks to low-$100ks. Net: modestly below the Oct. 30 market price of 0.25, at 0.23.

Lagno is shown 9th on the relevant Wikipedia FIDE rankings page as of the latest pre-cutoff revision, and Wikipedia’s chess ranking tables are monthly snapshots. That makes 2025-11-16 very likely to still reflect the same 1 November list. For 2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07, the question becomes whether she remains at least as good as rank 9 on the 1 Dec / 1 Feb lists. The key positive is strong recent form: she performed excellently in the 2025 Women’s Grand Swiss and qualified for the Candidates, suggesting her 2517 rating is not a fluke. The key negative is that her margin is thin—only 4 points over #10 Bibisara Assaubayeva and 12 over #11 Divya Deshmukh—so a single active month by a nearby rival could push her to 10th or lower. Overall this points to near-certainty for 11/16, a modestly-above-base-rate chance for 12/09, and a further-diluted but still >50% chance by 2/07.

My forecast is strongly NO across all three dates. As of the cutoff, Wikipedia had not flipped the sepsis entry, and the only additional context I found suggests sepsis is treated as a syndrome/dysregulated host response rather than a single-pathogen disease, with current research focused on immunotherapy and prevention of underlying infections rather than a direct sepsis vaccine. That makes a genuine vaccine-development breakthrough plus rapid Wikipedia recognition within 1 week to 3 months very unlikely. I allow a small increase over time because longer horizons give slightly more chance for a breakthrough or an editor reinterpretation, but the event remains rare.

As of the cutoff-era Wikipedia page, Bobby Finke is the listed long-course men's 1500m freestyle world-record holder at 14:30.67. The main question is whether anyone breaks that mark before each resolution date. The best additional context I found is reassuring: at the 2025 World Championships, the men's 1500m was won in 14:34.41, with Finke only third in 14:36.60, so the most recent top-level field was still several seconds slower than the record (search_0_result_6). The 2025 Worlds media guide also points to Daniel Wiffen's 14:34.07 as the recent world-title standard, again well off 14:30.67 (search_0_result_0). That suggests Finke's mark still had meaningful cushion entering mid-November 2025. I do leave some room for an upset because one extraordinary swim at a winter long-course meet could overturn it, and I did not fully verify the post-cutoff calendar; that risk accumulates with time, so the later dates are modestly closer to 0.5.

As of the cutoff, Wikipedia still lists Liu Zige as the women’s long-course 200m butterfly world-record holder. The key question is not whether the record is vulnerable in general, but whether it is likely to fall within 1 week, 1 month, or about 3 months. The main threat is Summer McIntosh: at the 2025 World Championships she swam 2:01.99, only 0.18 off Liu’s 2:01.81, and sources describe it as the second-fastest swim ever. That clearly makes this record more breakable than the generic swimming-record base rate. Still, the strongest recent attack window already occurred at a peak-tapered major championship in late July and the record survived. The remaining horizons are short, any new mark would also need ratification and Wikipedia reflection by the resolution date, and late-year swimming is often less favorable for long-course world-record attempts. So I assign a high chance Liu still holds the record on all three dates, but lower than the empirical 0.991 base rate and declining with horizon.
